The hOrseshOers' union met last
evening in musicians hail. Four
new members were initiated.


The following unions meet to-
night at Central Labor hall: l.aun-
dry workers, room /; plumbers,
room 2; BARRIERS, III painters hall;
timers, in musicians hall.


In the past % years 22,793 strikes
have occurred. OF these 1925 were
ordered by organized labor. In mum
Der of strikes New York leads with
6i60, more than twice as many as
any other state.


The session of the trades council
held last night is one of the short
est on record, The meeting lasted
no more than half an hour and, as
only a few delegates were present,
scarcely any business was trans
acted.


The board of aldermen of New
Brunswick. n. j., have passed an
ordinance making it obligatory that
the typographical union label be
printed on all envelopes and other
supplies of the city officials, and
| also providing that only papers
printing the label be recognized as
official advertising mediums.
| The plumbers union OF Dayton,
O.. on account of a long fight for
reasonable wages, has gone into
| business for itself and employs so
skilled workmen. The first month s
| business has amounted to $4,000,
After paying each man his wages
and meeting other expenses the
union has on hand $1,000.


! The building laborers of Tacoma
have formally organized themselves


into union owning allegiance to
the Federation of Labor The union


starts out with between 50 and &C
fun members and large number OF
applications. II is the intention tC


affiliate with the building trades


council of the city, and also to be
represented in the convention OF
the State Federation of Labor next
month in Seattle.
